channel.title: Artificial Intelligence on MinWeb
channel.description: Recent content in Artificial Intelligence on MinWeb

item.title: Allbirds Pivots to AI: Stock Soars 300% After GPU Shift
item.description:
If you’ve been feeling lately like you’ve accidentally slipped into a simulation written by a bored screenwriter who has run out of ideas, you aren’t alone. Just when we thought we’d seen the most bizarre corporate pivots in history, Allbirds—the brand famous for making cozy, sustainable wool sneakers—decided to hang up its shoelaces and chase the AI gold rush.

On April 15, 2026, the company rebranded to “NewBird AI.” They’re ditching footwear entirely to become a GPU-as-a-Service (GPUaaS) provider. Honestly, I’m still waiting for someone to tell me this is an elaborate April Fools’ joke that arrived two weeks late.

item.title: Iran Threatens to Destroy $30B Stargate AI Data Center
item.description: If the world of AI feels like a relentless, dizzying parade of corporate ego and billion-dollar announcements, you aren’t alone. For months, we’ve been hearing about “Stargate”—a massive, futuristic data center rising from the Abu Dhabi desert. It sounds like something pulled straight out of a sci-fi novel, a monument to the god-like computational power of OpenAI, Microsoft, and NVIDIA. Frankly, the sheer scale of the thing is enough to make anyone nervous.

item.title: Warhorse Studios Replaces Human Translator With AI to Save Costs
item.description:
It’s the kind of irony that feels scripted: a studio famous for its obsessive, historical dedication to the medieval Czech experience is now at the center of a debate about the “modern” way to cut corners.

If you are a fan of Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 (KCD2), you probably felt a distinct disconnect recently. On one hand, you have a record-breaking hit that brought the complexity of 15th-century Bohemia to life. On the other, there is a bitter conversation about how Warhorse Studios is treating the people who actually built that world.

item.title: Why OpenAI Is Shutting Down Sora: The Economics of Failure
item.description:
If you’ve been following the AI space, you’ve felt a distinct sense of whiplash lately. Yesterday, every tech company was racing to show off physics-defying video generation. Today, the headlines are all about a massive retreat.

On March 24, 2026, OpenAI pulled the plug on Sora. The shutdown is total: the standalone app, the developer API, and every video feature baked into ChatGPT are gone. For a product hyped as the “future of cinema,” its death feels shockingly sudden. But if you look past the slick marketing, the writing was on the wall for a long time.
